entries.seoTitle: Football Card Grading ROI Guide — When to Grade | CardSnap
entries.seoDescription: Should grade-or-skip Football cards? See ROI by condition/era/player.
entries.h1: Should You Grade Your Football Card?
entries.subtitle: This guide helps Football card collectors determine whether grading their cards is worth the investment. We explore key factors influencing grading decisions and potential ROI for various cards.
gradingLogic: Vintage Football cards from the 1970s and 1980s can see significant value increases when graded, especially if in excellent condition.
Rookie cards of star players like Tom Brady or Patrick Mahomes are often worth grading due to their demand and potential for high returns.
Autographed Football cards hold value that can greatly increase with a good grade, making grading a viable option.
Limited edition or short-printed Football cards should be graded to maximize their market value.
High-grade cards from recent championship seasons can command premium prices, making grading a smart choice.

keyCharacteristics.title: Condition
keyCharacteristics.desc: The condition of Football cards significantly impacts their value, with higher grades fetching much better prices.

keyCharacteristics.title: Player Popularity
keyCharacteristics.desc: Cards of high-profile players like Joe Burrow or Dak Prescott can see a substantial ROI when graded.

keyCharacteristics.title: Rarity
keyCharacteristics.desc: Rare cards, particularly those with limited print runs, can appreciate in value when graded.

keyCharacteristics.title: Era
keyCharacteristics.desc: Older cards often have a nostalgic value that can be enhanced through professional grading.

roiExamples.cardName: 2020 Panini Prizm Justin Herbert Rookie Card
roiExamples.reason: The demand for Herbert's rookie card is high, and grading can significantly increase its value.
While there is some ROI potential, the costs involved may not justify grading for all collectors.
The low raw value and high grading cost make this card not worth grading.

whenToGrade: When you have a high-demand rookie card from a popular player.
If your card is a limited edition or has a unique feature that collectors value.
For vintage cards in excellent condition that are likely to appreciate in value.
When you possess an autographed card that can significantly increase in value if graded.
If you have multiple copies of a card and want to maximize the value of the best condition copy.
skipGrading: When the raw value of the card is low and the grading cost exceeds potential returns.
For common cards that are widely available and have little collector interest.
If the card has significant wear or damage, which would yield a low grade.
When the market for that specific card is stagnant or declining.
entries.marketInsight: The Football card market has seen a resurgence in demand, particularly for rookie cards and autographed items. Grading remains a key strategy for maximizing ROI, but collectors should be discerning about which cards to submit.
